output.sparql:
PREFIX rdfs: <http://www.w3.org/2000/01/rdf-schema#>
PREFIX p: <http://www.wikidata.org/prop/>
PREFIX ps: <http://www.wikidata.org/prop/statement/>
PREFIX xsd: <http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema#>
PREFIX wd: <http://www.wikidata.org/entity/>
PREFIX pq: <http://www.wikidata.org/prop/qualifier/>
SELECT ?manager ?label WHERE {
  wd:Q18656 p:P286 ?statement .
  ?statement ps:P286 ?manager .
  ?statement pq:P580 ?start .
  ?statement pq:P582 ?end .
  FILTER(xsd:date(?start) <= "2017-12-31" ^^ xsd:date)
  FILTER(xsd:date(?end) >= "2017-01-01" ^^ xsd:date)
  ?manager rdfs:label ?label .
  FILTER(LANG(?label) = "en")
}
output.selections:
Using entities:
- Manchester United F.C. (wd:Q18656), also known as "Manchester United", "Manchester United FC", "Manchester United Football Club", "Man U", "Man United", etc.:
  - association football club in Manchester, England
  - is instance of association football club

Using properties:
- head coach (wd:P286 as p/ps), also known as "club manager", "coach", "manager", "senior coach", "team manager", etc.:
  - on-field manager or head coach of a sports club (not to be ... manager P505, which is not a coaching position) or person
- start time (wd:P580 as pq), also known as "air date", "began", "began airing", "began broadcasting", "began streaming", etc.:
  - time an entity begins to exist or a statement starts being valid
- end time (wd:P582 as pq), also known as "abolished", "cease date", "cease operation", "cease time", "closed", etc.:
  - moment when an entity ceases to exist and a statement stops being entirely valid or no longer be true

```sparql
PREFIX xsd: <http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema#>

SELECT ?manager ?label WHERE {
  wd:Q18656 p:P286 ?statement .
  ?statement ps:P286 ?manager .
  ?statement pq:P580 ?start .
  ?statement pq:P582 ?end .
  FILTER(xsd:date(?start) <= "2017-12-31" ^^ xsd:date)
  FILTER(xsd:date(?end) >= "2017-01-01" ^^ xsd:date)
  ?manager rdfs:label ?label .
  FILTER(LANG(?label) = "en")
}
```

messages.content:
You are a question answering assistant. Your job is to generate a SPARQL query to answer a given user question.

You should follow a step-by-step approach to generate the SPARQL query:
1. Determine possible entities and properties implied by the user question.
2. Search for the entities and properties in the knowledge graphs. Where applicable, constrain the searches with already identified entities and properties.
3. Gradually build up the SPARQL query using the identified entities and properties. Start with simple queries and add more complexity as needed. Execute intermediate queries to get feedback and to verify your assumptions. You may need to refine or rethink your current plan based on the query results and go back to step 2 if needed, possibly multiple times.
4. Use the answer or cancel function to finalize your answer and stop the generation process.

Available knowledge graphs:
- "wikidata" at https://qlever.dev/api/wikidata
  - "entity" index (type="fuzzy"): Entities indexed by their labels and synonyms
  - "property" index (type="embedding", modalities="text"): Properties indexed by their labels, synonyms, and identifiers

Index types used:
- "embedding": Retrieves items by cosine similarity between their embeddings and the query embedding. The embedding model used depends on the index and may support text, images, or both.
- "fuzzy": Retrieves items by overlap between their label words and the query keywords. The query keywords must not match label words exactly, but some fuzziness is allowed. The longer a query keyword is, the more it can deviate from a label word and still be considered a match, though it will also contribute less to the overall score. No special query operators like AND/OR are supported.

SPARQL prefixes for use in function calls:
- bd: http://www.bigdata.com/rdf#
- cc: http://creativecommons.org/ns#
- data: https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/Special:EntityData/
- dc: http://purl.org/dc/elements/1.1/
- dct: http://purl.org/dc/terms/
- foaf: http://xmlns.com/foaf/0.1/
- geo: http://www.opengis.net/ont/geosparql#
- geof: http://www.opengis.net/def/function/geosparql/
- gn: http://www.geonames.org/ontology#
- hint: http://www.bigdata.com/queryHints#

- math: http://www.w3.org/2005/xpath-functions/math#

- p: http://www.wikidata.org/prop/
- pq: http://www.wikidata.org/prop/qualifier/
- pqn: http://www.wikidata.org/prop/qualifier/value-normalized/

- prn: http://www.wikidata.org/prop/reference/value-normalized/

- ql: http://qlever.cs.uni-freiburg.de/builtin-functions/
- qlss: https://qlever.cs.uni-freiburg.de/spatialSearch/
- rdf: http://www.w3.org/1999/02/22-rdf-syntax-ns#
- rdfs: http://www.w3.org/2000/01/rdf-schema#
- ref: http://www.wikidata.org/reference/

- schema: http://schema.org/
- skos: http://www.w3.org/2004/02/skos/core#

- void: http://rdfs.org/ns/void#

- wdt: http://www.wikidata.org/prop/direct/
- wdtn: http://www.wikidata.org/prop/direct-normalized/
- wikibase: http://wikiba.se/ontology#
- xsd: http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema#

Additional rules to follow:
- Explain your thought process before and after each step and function call.
- Do not ask the user for clarification until you have made an initial attempt at completing the task. When the task input is incomplete or ambiguous, proceed based on reasonable assumptions.
- Do not use "SERVICE wikibase:label { bd:serviceParam wikibase:language ..." in SPARQL queries. It is unsupported by the used QLever SPARQL endpoints. Use rdfs:label or similar properties to get labels instead.
- Always execute your final SPARQL query before giving an answer to make sure it returns the expected results.
- The SPARQL query should always return the actual identifiers / IRIs of the items in its result. It additionally may return labels or other human-readable information, but they are optional and should be put within optional clauses unless explicitly requested by the user.
- Do not stop early if there are still obvious improvements to be made to the SPARQL query. For example, keep refining your SPARQL query if its result contains irrelevant items or is missing items you expected.
- Do not perform additional computation (e.g. filtering, sorting, calculations) on the result of the SPARQL query to determine the answer. All computation should be done solely within SPARQL.
- For questions with a "True" or "False" answer the SPARQL query should be an ASK query.

reasoning.content:
Okay, let's tackle this problem. The user is asking for the name of the manager of Manchester United F.C. in 2017.

First, I need to confirm which entity for Manchester United we're dealing with. From the previous search_entity response, the main one is wd:Q18656 (Manchester United F.C.). So that's our club.

Next, they want the manager in 2017. In sports contexts, "manager" can sometimes refer to the head coach, especially in European football. The search_property_of_entity result shows that "head coach" (wdt:P286) is listed as a property for manager. So maybe P286 is the correct property here.

Now, checking the timeline. Jose Mourinho was the manager of Manchester United from 2016 to 2018. So 2017 falls within his tenure. But we need to confirm via the data.

I should construct a SPARQL query to get the manager (using P286) for Manchester United F.C. (Q18656) with a time constraint for 2017.

The challenge is handling the time. In Wikidata, for positions held, they often use qualifiers like start time (P580) and end time (P582). So the property P286 might have qualifiers for the start and end dates.

So the structure would be:
- Subject: Manchester United F.C. (wd:Q18656)
- Property: head coach (wdt:P286) which points to the manager (e.g., Jose Mourinho)
- But need to check the qualifiers on the statement to see if the time period includes 2017.

So the SPARQL needs to:
1. Find all instances where the subject is Q18656, property is P286.
2. Check the qualifiers for start and end dates to see if 2017 is within that range.

Possible approach:
SELECT ?manager ?managerLabel WHERE {
  wd:Q18656 wdt:P286 ?manager.
  ?statement ps:P286 ?manager;
             pq:P580 ?start;
             pq:P582 ?end.
  FILTER(?start <= "2017-01-01"^^xsd:date && ?end >= "2017-12-31"^^xsd:date)
  SERVICE wikibase:label { bd:serviceParam wikibase:language "en". }
}

Wait, but the user mentioned not to use SERVICE wikibase:label. So labels need to be handled with rdfs:label or similar.

Alternative:
SELECT ?manager ?label WHERE {
  wd:Q18656 p:P286 ?statement.
  ?statement ps:P286 ?manager.
  ?statement pq:P580 ?start.
  ?statement pq:P582 ?end.
  ?manager rdfs:label ?label.
  FILTER(LANG(?label) = "en")
  FILTER(xsd:date(?start) <= "2017-01-01"^^xsd:date && xsd:date(?end) >= "2017-12-31"^^xsd:date)
}

But need to check if the dates are correctly formatted. Also, sometimes end dates might not be present if the person is still in the role, but since 2017 is historical, we should have end dates.

Wait, Jose Mourinho left in December 2018, so his end date might be 2018-12-18. So for 2017, the period should be covered.

Another point: In Wikidata, sometimes P39 (position held) is used for manager roles, but the search_property_of_entity gave P286 as the relevant property. Let's verify.

Looking back: search_property_of_entity with entity Q18656 and query "manager" gave "head coach (wd:P286 as wdt/p)".

So P286 is the property to use. So the direct property is wdt:P286 for current coach, but historical data may be stored with statements that have start and end dates.

So the correct approach is to use the property P286 as a statement (p:P286) to get all instances, then filter by time.

So the query would use the p: and ps: and pq: prefixes.

Putting it all together:

SELECT ?manager ?managerLabel WHERE {
  wd:Q18656 p:P286 ?statement .
  ?statement ps:P286 ?manager .
  ?statement pq:P580 ?start .
  ?statement pq:P582 ?end .
  FILTER (xsd:dateTime(?start) <= "2017-01-01T00:00:00Z"^^xsd:dateTime)
  FILTER (xsd:dateTime(?end) >= "2017-12-31T23:59:59Z"^^xsd:dateTime)
  ?manager rdfs:label ?managerLabel .
  FILTER (LANG(?managerLabel) = "en")
}

Wait, but the user said not to use SERVICE wikibase:label, so using rdfs:label with language filter.

However, in practice, the rdfs:label might not have the preferred label, but it's the best we can do.

Alternatively, maybe ?manager wdt:P31 wd:Q5 to ensure it's a person, but the user didn't ask for that.

Now, checking if there are any issues. The main issue is whether the start and end dates are recorded correctly in the qualifiers for P286.

Another thing: In some cases, there might be multiple statements, so we need to filter for the correct year.

Let me think if 2017 is specifically covered. Mourinho's tenure was from May 2016 to December 2018, so yes, 2017 is within that range.

But the query needs to check that the period includes any part of 2017, but to be precise, since it's a year, the date range should overlap with 2017.

The filters here are checking if the start is <= 2017-01-01 and end >= 2017-12-31, which would mean the entire year 2017 is within the tenure. But what if the manager started in 2017? Then the start would be 2017-01-01 to 2017-12-31.

Wait, the correct way to check overlap is:

start <= 2017-12-31 AND end >= 2017-01-01

So changing the filters to:

FILTER(xsd:date(?start) <= "2017-12-31"^^xsd:date && xsd:date(?end) >= "2017-01-01"^^xsd:date)

That way, any period that overlaps with 2017 is included.

Yes, because if a manager was hired on 2016-12-01 and fired on 2017-01-01, they were manager for part of 2017.

So adjusting the filters.

So the updated query:

SELECT ?manager ?managerLabel WHERE {
  wd:Q18656 p:P286 ?statement .
  ?statement ps:P286 ?manager .
  ?statement pq:P580 ?start .
  ?statement pq:P582 ?end .
  FILTER (xsd:date(?start) <= "2017-12-31"^^xsd:date)
  FILTER (xsd:date(?end) >= "2017-01-01"^^xsd:date)
  ?manager rdfs:label ?managerLabel .
  FILTER (LANG(?managerLabel) = "en")
}

But in SPARQL for Wikidata, sometimes the dates are in different formats. Maybe need to use STRSTARTS or other functions, but it's safer to cast to xsd:date.

Wait, in QLever, which is the engine used here, the date handling might differ. But the user says it's QLever at qlever.dev/api/wikidata.

QLever allows comparing dates as strings, but it's better to use xsd:date.

But maybe the actual data in Wikidata has dates in ISO format, so comparing as strings might work.

Alternatively, using year extraction.

But perhaps the standard way is to convert to xsd:date.

Another thing: The qualifiers P580 and P582 might not exist for all statements. For example, if a manager is still current, but in this case, we know it's 2017, so there should be an end date.

But to be safe, maybe check that both start and end exist.

Wait, the user is asking specifically for 2017, so both dates should be present (since 2017 is in the past), but in case a manager was appointed during 2017, the start date would be in 2017.

So including both start and end in the statement is necessary.

So the query should include those qualifiers.

Now, let's test if this query works by executing it.

But since I can't actually run it now, I need to predict.

Assuming that the data is correctly recorded, and P286 is the correct property.

Wait, looking back at the search_property_of_entity result, the first property is head coach (P286). So that's the one to use.

Another check: What is the actual property for Manchester United's manager in 2017? According to knowledge, it's Jose Mourinho. So the query should return him.

Let me structure the SPARQL:

PREFIX wd: <http://www.wikidata.org/entity/>
PREFIX wdt: <http://www.wikidata.org/prop/direct/>
PREFIX p: <http://www.wikidata.org/prop/>
PREFIX pq: <http://www.wikidata.org/prop/qualifier/>

SELECT ?manager ?label WHERE {
  wd:Q18656 p:P286 ?statement .
  ?statement ps:P286 ?manager .
  ?statement pq:P580 ?start .
  ?statement pq:P582 ?end .
  FILTER (?start <= "2017-12-31T00:00:00Z"^^xsd:dateTime)
  FILTER (?end >= "2017-01-01T00:00:00Z"^^xsd:dateTime)
  ?manager rdfs:label ?label .
  FILTER (LANG(?label) = "en")
}

Wait, the FILTERs need to be in the correct format. Maybe using strings:

FILTER (STR(?start) <= "2017-12-31")
FILTER (STR(?end) >= "2017-01-01")

But better to cast.

Alternatively, using year:

FILTER(year(xsd:date(?start)) <= 2017)
FILTER(year(xsd:date(?end)) >= 2017)

But that might not be precise, as some tenures could end mid-2017.

Wait, better to check the exact date range.

Another possibility: Use the time precision. Wikidata has precision (P1083), so maybe the start and end dates have year precision only.

But for accuracy, better to compare as dates.

In any case, the correct SPARQL should check for overlap between the tenure and 2017.

Now, putting all together.

Also, need to ensure that the results are for the correct football club (wd:Q18656).

Finally, the answer should be the name, so the label of the manager.

Possible errors: If the property used is incorrect. For example, maybe in Wikidata, they use P57 (director) for film, but for football managers, it's P286.

Alternatively, perhaps for soccer clubs, the position held uses P39 with a qualifier.

Wait, another way: Maybe the manager is listed under P570 (but that's date of death), no.

Alternatively, check if "Manchester United F.C." has a P338 (instance of) which is Q476028 (association football club), which it does.

Perhaps another approach: Find all people with occupation P286 (head coach) for the club.

But according to previous steps, P286 is the property for the club's head coach.

So I think the initial approach is correct.

Now, the final SPARQL should retrieve Jose Mourinho's label.

So the answer would be "José Mourinho" but need to check the label.

In Wikidata, his name is "José Mourinho" but perhaps with en label.

So the query should return that.

Now, construct the SPARQL accordingly.

tool_calls.name: execute

args.sparql: SELECT ?manager ?label WHERE { wd:Q18656 p:P286 ?statement . ?statement ps:P286 ?manager . ?statement pq:P580 ?start . ?statement pq:P582 ?end . FILTER(xsd:date(?start) <= "2017-12-31"^^xsd:date) FILTER(xsd:date(?end) >= "2017-01-01"^^xsd:date) ?manager rdfs:label ?label . FILTER(LANG(?label) = "en") }

| manager                   | label                   |
| ------------------------- | ----------------------- |
| José Mourinho (wd:Q79983) | José Mourinho (lang:en) |

reasoning.content:
Okay, let's break this down. The user is asking for the name of the manager of Manchester United F.C. in 2017.

First, I need to confirm which entity in Wikidata corresponds to Manchester United F.C. I called the search_entity function with "Manchester United F.C." and got several results. The top one was wd:Q18656, which is the main club entity. The others were categories or related entities, so I focused on wd:Q18656.

Next, I needed to find the property that represents the manager. Since the user mentioned "manager," I used search_property_of_entity on wd:Q18656 with the query "manager." The top result was "head coach" (wd:P286), which in football contexts is often synonymous with manager, especially in European clubs. I noticed that "head coach" is the property used here, even though the user says "manager."

To get the manager in 2017, I had to consider that managerial roles have start and end dates. The property p:P286 is for statements of the head coach role. Each statement has qualifiers like P580 (start time) and P582 (end time). So I structured the SPARQL query to find the head coach where the start date is on or before December 31, 2017, and the end date is on or after January 1, 2017. This ensures the person was manager throughout 2017.

I used FILTERs with xsd:date to compare dates properly. Also, since the user wants the name, I added rdfs:label to get the English label for the manager's entity. The query returned José Mourinho. Checking real-world knowledge, he was indeed the manager of Manchester United during 2017, so the result makes sense.

I should verify if there were other managers that year. Maybe an interim? But the query returned only one row, José Mourinho, which matches what I know. So the answer is correct.

args.sparql: SELECT ?manager ?label WHERE { wd:Q18656 p:P286 ?statement . ?statement ps:P286 ?manager . ?statement pq:P580 ?start . ?statement pq:P582 ?end . FILTER(xsd:date(?start) <= "2017-12-31"^^xsd:date) FILTER(xsd:date(?end) >= "2017-01-01"^^xsd:date) ?manager rdfs:label ?label . FILTER(LANG(?label) = "en") }
args.answer: The manager of Manchester United F.C. in 2017 was José Mourinho.

question: What is the name of the manager in 2017 of the soccer club Manchester United F.C.
